summary:
Mussolini's March on Rome of October 27–30, 1922, was the event that brought the Italian Fascist Party (PNF) to power in Italy and made Benito Mussolini prime minister. Fascist Blackshirt units converged on Rome from several directions, while the liberal government of Luigi Facta hesitated. King Victor Emmanuel III, facing the choice between authorizing the army to suppress the march or inviting Mussolini to form a government, chose the latter. On October 29, 1922, he summoned Mussolini to Rome by telegram and asked him to form a government.

The March on Rome was in significant part a political bluff: the Fascist militia forces were poorly armed and disorganized, and the Italian army could almost certainly have dispersed them if ordered to do so. The king's decision not to sign a declaration of martial law and instead to capitulate to Fascist pressure opened the door to Mussolini's consolidation of power. Over the following two years, Mussolini used legal and extralegal means to transform Italy's constitutional monarchy into a one-party dictatorship.

The March on Rome established a template that influenced subsequent fascist and authoritarian movements: the use of paramilitary street violence combined with sufficient legitimacy to paralyze liberal institutions and persuade conservative elites that fascists could be brought into partnership to protect their interests. Mussolini's success demonstrated that fascism could take power through a combination of intimidation and manipulation rather than outright revolution.
